nativeRange.habitat: Coastal lagoon and seaward reef habitats with rocky substrates and coral structures; typically found in small caves, narrow crevices, under ledges, and occasionally in surge zones, generally at depths of roughly 1–30 m

diet.notes: Carnivorous ambush predator that has been documented feeding primarily on small shrimps and crabs in the wild. In captivity, individuals tend to initially accept only live foods (saltwater feeder shrimp are commonly recommended for first feedings); with patience, many can be transitioned to meaty frozen foods such as krill, silversides, mysis, and chopped crustacean flesh. Target-feeding with a feeding stick is generally advised to help wean specimens onto prepared foods. Ornamental shrimp and small fish are at high risk of predation.

avoidWith: small fish that fit in mouth
other lionfish unless adequate space provided

summary: The Radiata Lionfish (Pterois radiata), also known as the Clearfin or Tailbar Lionfish, is a striking Indo-Pacific scorpionfish marked by reddish-brown vertical bars, long feathery pectoral fins, and distinctive horizontal white tail stripes. Generally considered intermediate care; tends to be nocturnal and benefits from ample crevices and ledges in a well-established saltwater system.
careNotes:
The Radiata Lionfish is distributed broadly across the Indo-Pacific, from the Red Sea and East Africa east to the Society Islands, north to the Ryukyu Islands of Japan, and south to New Caledonia. It generally inhabits rocky reefs and lagoon environments at depths of roughly 1 to 30 m, hiding in caves, crevices, and under ledges during the day. It is recognized by the long, blank (unspotted) pectoral and dorsal fin spines and the pair of horizontal white stripes on the caudal peduncle that distinguish it from congeners such as Pterois volitans and Pterois antennata.

Venomous spines: The dorsal, pelvic, and anal fin spines are venomous and capable of inflicting a painful sting. Stings have been compared to a bee or wasp sting; soaking the affected area in hot water (as hot as can be tolerated, around 40–45°C) is commonly recommended, and medical attention is advisable for severe reactions or known allergies.

Nocturnal behavior: This species is mainly nocturnal and emerges from cover at dusk to hunt. Subdued lighting, dawn/dusk transition periods, and evening feedings tend to encourage more natural activity and feeding response.

Feeding transition: Many specimens initially refuse prepared foods; patience and gradual weaning from live to frozen meaty foods is generally advised. Feeding 2–3 times per week is commonly considered sufficient for adults to avoid overfeeding and excess waste.

Reef compatibility: Generally considered 'reef-safe with caution' — it tends to leave corals and sessile invertebrates undisturbed but will readily consume ornamental shrimp, small crustaceans, and any fish small enough to fit in its mouth.

Cross-source notes: Adult-size cited by LiveAquaria as up to 10 in (25.4 cm) while FishBase, Wikipedia, and AquaticCommunity report 24 cm maximum (commonly ~20 cm); the 20–24 cm range used reflects this consensus. Minimum tank size ranged from 55 gal / 208 L (AquaticCommunity) to 70 gal / 265 L (LiveAquaria); 300 L (~80 gal) was selected to bias toward the cautious side given the species' adult size, ambush-predator behavior, and bottom-dwelling territoriality. Note: FishBase has reassigned this species to the genus Pteropterus (P. radiatus, 2023); the trade and most aquarium sources continue to use Pterois radiata.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has rarely been documented. As with other Pterois species, wild reproduction is understood to involve pelagic broadcast spawning following courtship displays, with buoyant egg masses released into the water column. Captive spawning is uncommon outside of public aquarium settings.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Provided care level (moderate), temperament (semi-aggressive), reef compatibility (with caution), pH (8.1–8.4), specific gravity (1.020–1.025), temperature (72–78°F / 22.2–25.5°C), dKH (8–12), max size (10 in), and minimum tank (70 gal / 265 L). Also noted venomous spines and Florida shipping restriction.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed scientific identity (currently catalogued as Pteropterus radiatus, formerly Pterois radiata), Scorpaenidae family, distribution from Red Sea to Society Islands and Ryukyus to New Caledonia, max length 24 cm TL (common 20 cm), depth 1–30 m, preferred temperature 25.1–29.3°C, exclusive diet of small crabs and shrimps, IUCN Least Concern.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Confirmed common names (Clearfin/Tailbar Lionfish, Radial Firefish, Fireworks Fish), 24 cm max size, 12–13 venomous dorsal spines plus 3 anal spines, mainly nocturnal behavior, and habitat preferences. Used for taxonomic and morphological detail only — not used for husbandry parameters.

additional.name: AquaticCommunity
additional.notes: Cross-checked min tank (55 gal / 208 L), temperature 75–82°F (23.9–27.8°C), pH 8.1–8.4, salinity 1.020–1.025, max size ~24 cm. Used as a third hobby-source confirmation of water parameters and the broader temperature range.

fish.breedingNotes: Pelagic broadcast spawning has been documented in the wild following courtship displays; captive spawning is rarely reported outside of public aquarium settings.
